meta.title: 4.2 Forms and User Input
meta.description: How React handles what users type.

blocks.prompt: In regular HTML, when you type in an input field, the browser handles everything -- it shows what you typed and keeps track of the value. In React, the component manages its own state. So how do you think React keeps an input field in sync with a state variable? Does the input manage itself, or does React tell it what to show?

blocks.question: A React form has `<form onSubmit={handleSubmit}>` but the handleSubmit function does NOT call `e.preventDefault()`. What happens when the user clicks the submit button?
blocks.answer: The entire page reloads. The browser performs its default form submission behavior -- an HTTP request that navigates to a new page (or reloads the current one). In a React single-page app, this means all state is lost and the app restarts from scratch. The form data may or may not reach the server depending on the setup, but even if it does, the user experience is broken.

options: A) The input has `value={email}` but no `onChange` handler. React forces the input to show the current state (empty string) and the user's keystrokes are ignored.
B) The useState should initialize with null instead of an empty string.
C) The form needs an onSubmit handler before inputs will work.
D) The input type "email" is not supported in React.
problems.explanation: This is the classic frozen input bug. The `value` attribute locks the input to whatever the state is (empty string). Without an `onChange` handler, no keystrokes update the state, so the input stays empty. The fix: add `onChange={(e) => setEmail(e.target.value)}`.

options: A) `handleSubmit` does not call `e.preventDefault()`. The browser performs a default form submission (page reload) before the fetch can complete, so the login never succeeds.
B) The fetch URL is wrong.
C) localStorage.setItem does not work in React.
D) The password input type prevents submission.
problems.explanation: The handleSubmit function does not receive or use the event object, so `e.preventDefault()` is never called. When the form submits, the browser reloads the page before the fetch has a chance to complete. The fix: change to `function handleSubmit(e) { e.preventDefault(); ... }`. The white flash is the page reloading. This is the most common AI-generated form bug.

options: A) Each onChange replaces the entire settings object with only one field. When email changes, `setSettings({ email: 'new' })` erases displayName and bio. Fix: spread the existing state first: `setSettings({ ...settings, email: e.target.value })`.
B) The textarea cannot be controlled in React.
C) The form needs an onSubmit handler for the inputs to work.
D) useState cannot hold objects.
problems.explanation: This is the same bug from Module 3.2 in a form context. `setSettings({ email: e.target.value })` replaces the entire state object with `{ email: 'new@example.com' }` -- the displayName and bio keys are gone. The fix: spread the existing state first in every onChange: `setSettings({ ...settings, email: e.target.value })`. Or better yet, use the shared handler pattern with `[e.target.name]: e.target.value`. You would tell the AI: "Each onChange handler replaces the entire settings object. Spread the existing settings before overriding the changed field."

blocks.prompt: Explain what a controlled component is in React. What two attributes on an input make it controlled? What happens if you have `value` but no `onChange`? And why is `e.preventDefault()` essential in form submit handlers?

blocks.content: React forms use controlled components where state drives the input's value and onChange updates the state on every keystroke. Missing onChange means a frozen input. Missing e.preventDefault() in the submit handler means a full page reload. These are the two most common form bugs, especially in AI-generated code.

blocks.prompt: Every form you fill out on the web -- login pages, search bars, checkout flows, settings panels -- uses these patterns under the hood. The next time you encounter a form that does not work right (input does not respond, page refreshes on submit), you now know exactly where to look.

reviewQuestions.question: What two attributes make an input a 'controlled component' in React?
options: value and onChange
name and type
placeholder and defaultValue
reviewQuestions.explanation: A controlled component uses 'value' to display the current state and 'onChange' to update the state on every keystroke. Together, React drives the input.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's it! value + onChange is the controlled component formula.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The answer is value and onChange. The value prop tells the input what to show, and onChange updates state when the user types.

reviewQuestions.question: What does e.preventDefault() do in a form's onSubmit handler?
options: It clears the form fields
It stops the browser from reloading the page
It validates the form data
reviewQuestions.explanation: By default, submitting an HTML form causes a full page reload. e.preventDefault() stops that default behavior so React can handle the submission without losing state.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Nice work! Without preventDefault, the page reloads and all React state is lost.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Not quite -- e.preventDefault() stops the browser's default form submission behavior, which is to reload the entire page. This is essential in React apps.

reviewQuestions.question: If an input has 'value={email}' but no onChange handler, what happens when the user tries to type?
options: The input works normally
The input appears frozen -- keystrokes are ignored
The input crashes the app
reviewQuestions.explanation: Without onChange, the state never updates. But value locks the input to the current state (empty string), so the user's keystrokes have no visible effect. The input appears frozen.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Correct! This is the classic frozen input bug -- a missing onChange handler.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Actually, the input appears frozen. The value prop locks it to the current state, and without onChange, nothing ever updates that state.
